gold.rationale: The article's central claim is fabricated: the cited rumor-control notice states that reports of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda being 'fully dismantled and repaired on the ground' are false, and that current work consists of deformation monitoring, comprehensive surveying, information-model building, and partial reinforcement. The visuals further mislead by using synthetic and edited-looking scenes to make the nonexistent dismantling plan appear real.

atomic_points.post_summary: Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda in northern Shanxi province to address long-term structural issues.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda in northern Shanxi province, setting in motion a rare intervention on one of the world's oldest and tallest surviving timber towers after years of structural assessment and debate over how best to preserve it.
atoms: Chinese cultural heritage authorities approved a full dismantling of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda.
The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is located in northern Shanxi province.
The project involves a ground-level overhaul of the pagoda.
The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is one of the world's oldest surviving timber towers.
The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is one of the world's tallest surviving timber towers.
The decision follows years of structural assessment.
The decision followed debate over preservation methods.

sentence_level.sentence_text: According to cultural heritage planning materials reviewed by people familiar with the project, the decision marks a turn away from earlier reinforcement-led efforts toward a complete dismantling-and-repair operation.
atoms: Cultural heritage planning materials were reviewed by people familiar with the project.
The decision marks a shift from earlier reinforcement-led efforts.
The project is now a complete dismantling-and-repair operation.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The aim is to deal with long-term deformation, accumulated structural stress and what specialists see as the limits of piecemeal stabilization.
atoms: The aim is to address long-term deformation.
The aim is to address accumulated structural stress.
Specialists view piecemeal stabilization as having reached its limits.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Preservation experts involved in consultations said the plan envisions taking the pagoda down section by section, restoring it at ground level and then reassembling it after damaged or weakened members are treated or replaced under strict conservation protocols.
atoms: Preservation experts were involved in consultations regarding the plan.
The plan envisions taking the pagoda down section by section.
The pagoda will be restored at ground level.
The pagoda will be reassembled after damaged members are treated.
The pagoda will be reassembled after weakened members are replaced.
The work will follow strict conservation protocols.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Several people with knowledge of the process said local officials in Yingxian and figures in provincial heritage circles have been preparing for the move for months.
atoms: Local officials in Yingxian have been preparing for the move for months.
Figures in provincial heritage circles have been preparing for the move for months.
Several people with knowledge of the process provided this information.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That followed surveys, digital mapping and engineering reviews that concluded a full disassembly offered the safest long-term path for protecting the monument.
atoms: Surveys were conducted as part of the assessment process.
Digital mapping was conducted as part of the assessment process.
Engineering reviews were conducted as part of the assessment process.
The assessments concluded that full disassembly offers the safest long-term path.
The goal is protecting the monument.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The project has not yet been fully opened to public view, but site preparations, access controls and materials planning are understood to be under way before a formal construction phase begins.
atoms: The project has not yet been fully opened to public view.
Site preparations are underway.
Access controls are being implemented.
Materials planning is underway.
These activities are occurring before the formal construction phase begins.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, also known as the Sakyamuni Pagoda of Fogong Temple, was built in 1056 during the Liao dynasty and is widely regarded as a masterpiece of Chinese timber architecture.
atoms: The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is also known as the Sakyamuni Pagoda of Fogong Temple.
The pagoda was built in 1056.
The pagoda was built during the Liao dynasty.
The pagoda is widely regarded as a masterpiece of Chinese timber architecture.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Standing about 67 meters high, it has long attracted architects, historians and tourists because of its sophisticated bracket systems, layered internal structure and unusual survival through centuries of earthquakes, warfare and exposure to the elements.
atoms: The pagoda stands about 67 meters high.
The pagoda has attracted architects, historians, and tourists.
The pagoda features sophisticated bracket systems.
The pagoda has a layered internal structure.
The pagoda survived centuries of earthquakes.
The pagoda survived centuries of warfare.
The pagoda survived exposure to the elements.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Specialists say its importance extends beyond China, describing it as a singular reference point for the study of large-scale premodern wooden construction.
atoms: Specialists say the pagoda's importance extends beyond China.
The pagoda is described as a singular reference point.
The reference point is for the study of large-scale premodern wooden construction.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That standing has only intensified concern over the building's condition.
atoms: The pagoda's status has intensified concern over its condition.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Researchers and conservation planners have spent years recording tilt, localized distortion, stress in key load-bearing members and the aging of timber connections across several levels.
atoms: Researchers and conservation planners have spent years recording the pagoda's condition.
They recorded tilt in the structure.
They recorded localized distortion.
They recorded stress in key load-bearing members.
They recorded aging of timber connections across several levels.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Monitoring campaigns using laser scanning, total-station survey equipment, photogrammetry and high-resolution digital modeling have created a detailed record of minute changes in the structure.
atoms: Monitoring campaigns were conducted using laser scanning.
Monitoring campaigns were conducted using total-station survey equipment.
Monitoring campaigns were conducted using photogrammetry.
Monitoring campaigns were conducted using high-resolution digital modeling.
These campaigns created a detailed record of minute changes in the structure.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Experts involved in the assessment said those studies, together with on-site inspections and historical analysis of earlier repairs, formed the basis for the decision to dismantle it.
atoms: Experts involved in the assessment provided this information.
The studies formed the basis for the decision to dismantle the pagoda.
On-site inspections formed the basis for the decision.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Under the current technical plan, the overhaul is expected to unfold in carefully sequenced stages.
atoms: The overhaul is expected to unfold in carefully sequenced stages under the current technical plan.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Workers would begin by installing external protective systems and temporary supports.
atoms: Workers will begin by installing external protective systems.
Workers will begin by installing temporary supports.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Architectural elements and structural members would then be removed in an order designed to preserve load paths and historic fabric.
atoms: Architectural elements will be removed in a specific order.
Structural members will be removed in a specific order.
The removal order is designed to preserve load paths.
The removal order is designed to preserve historic fabric.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Each timber component is to be photographed, measured, tagged and cataloged before being moved to ground-level conservation zones, where specialists will check for cracking, compression, biological deterioration and traces of past repairs.

Each timber component will be measured.
Each timber component will be tagged.
Each timber component will be cataloged.

Specialists will check for cracking.

The models were generated during recent mapping campaigns.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Specialists said that database is expected to guide both dismantling and reassembly by allowing conservators to track the exact position, condition and treatment history of individual beams, columns, bracket arms and connecting elements.

sentence_level.sentence_text: They said the method reflects a broader trend in heritage engineering, in which detailed information models are used to support interventions on highly complex historic structures.
atoms: Specialists said the method reflects a broader trend in heritage engineering.
The trend involves using detailed information models.
The models are used to support interventions on highly complex historic structures.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Cover image: A wide view of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda shows cranes and scaffold towers positioned around the upper tiers as preservation teams prepare for the staged dismantling approved under the overhaul plan.

paragraph_level: Chinese authorities have approved a controversial full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda to address long-term structural deformation and stress.
The decision represents a shift from previous reinforcement efforts to a complete disassembly, repair, and reassembly strategy under strict conservation protocols.
Extensive monitoring campaigns using laser scanning, digital modeling, and engineering reviews concluded that full disassembly offers the safest long-term path for the monument's preservation.
The pagoda, built in 1056 during the Liao dynasty, is a 67-meter tall masterpiece of Chinese timber architecture and a singular reference point for premodern wooden construction.
The overhaul involves a carefully sequenced process of protective installation, component removal, cataloging, ground-level restoration, and precise reassembly guided by a digital inventory.
The project has sparked debate among conservation professionals regarding the risks of losing historical integrity versus the necessity of addressing decades of accumulated structural problems.
Tourists and local businesses are closely watching the process, hoping for limited viewing or interpretation exhibits during the expected multi-year closure period.
The dismantling phase is expected to begin after site mobilization and will likely last several years, involving temporary closure and tighter management of surrounding areas.

retrieved_evidence.text: In recent reports about the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda in Shanxi, protective scaffolding, cranes and safety barriers have been described around the multi‑storey timber structure, with workers and heritage technicians appearing to prepare for more intensive conservation measures. The Yingxian pagoda, dating back to the Liao dynasty and often cited as one of China’s oldest surviving all‑wooden towers, has long been monitored for structural deformation, leaning and weathering. According to local cultural relics authorities, emergency reinforcement zones and controlled access areas are common preliminary steps when major preservation or potential dismantling work is being considered for such nationally protected monuments.

According to accounts circulating among some local observers in recent days, heritage authorities are said to have opted for a comprehensive dismantling-and-repair campaign for the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, rather than limiting work to partial reinforcement. People familiar with internal discussions suggest that the proposed approach would involve intervening across the entire structure in a coordinated conservation programme. Officials have not released detailed timetables, and they emphasise that any extensive work would be subject to further expert consultation and approval procedures at higher levels.

Technical accounts relating to the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda indicate that any dismantling for conservation would require meticulous engineering preparation near the base and along the upper tiers. Descriptions mention suspended timber segments, temporary working platforms, numbered braces and heavy engineering equipment positioned around load‑bearing points, suggesting a cataloguing of components and stress redistribution before any element is removed. According to cultural heritage engineers familiar with wooden pagodas in Shanxi, each beam, bracket arm and column would typically be tagged and documented so that later reassembly can follow the original structural logic and satisfy national relic protection standards.

The Yingxian Wooden Pagoda in Shanxi province is being highlighted by heritage experts in recent reports as a landmark timber structure dating back to the 11th century, according to officials with the local cultural relics authority. The multi-storey Buddhist tower, built entirely of wood, is regarded as an important symbol of traditional Chinese architecture and religious history in northern China. Researchers note that the monument embodies sophisticated structural techniques from the Liao period and has been repeatedly cited in academic studies as a key reference point for the study of ancient wooden construction in East Asia.

Preservation specialists and academic observers have in recent days discussed possible approaches to safeguarding the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, with particular attention to whether dismantling would be the safest long-term option, according to people following the debate. Some experts cited in local media are described as favouring a comprehensive dismantling-and-reassembly strategy to address hidden structural risks, while others argue for more limited reinforcement measures in order to minimise intervention in the original fabric. Participants say the discussion is ongoing and that no single view has yet gained universal support.

Local authorities in Shanxi province are said to be considering temporary adjustments to visitor access at the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda in connection with anticipated conservation work, according to tourism officials. Under the scenarios currently being discussed, the site could be fully closed or subject to stricter entry controls for a certain period, in order to ensure safety and to facilitate technical operations. Travel agencies have been informally advised that arrangements may change at short notice once a final schedule for the project is confirmed.

Cultural heritage specialists in Shanxi have recently reiterated that the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is facing long-term preservation pressures, according to information released by local authorities. Experts involved in routine monitoring say that the ancient timber structure shows signs of gradual deformation and ongoing structural stress, issues they describe as not uncommon for large-scale wooden monuments of this age. The situation is said to require sustained conservation efforts, including careful assessment of load-bearing components and measures to mitigate the impact of environmental changes over the coming years.

Early descriptions of a proposed conservation project for the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda suggest that it would be carried out in successive stages, according to individuals briefed on preliminary plans. The scheme is said to involve dismantling the timber structure section by section, moving each part to ground level for restoration work, and later reassembling the tower once repairs are completed. People familiar with the discussions stress that such an operation would require meticulous documentation, labelling and handling of individual components, and that the technical details remain under study.

Archival descriptions of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, also known as the Sakyamuni Pagoda of Fogong Temple, highlight its status as one of China’s most important historic wooden monuments well before any recent restoration plans. Travel accounts and conservation surveys portray a tall octagonal structure rising above the surrounding county town, with multiple eaves, intricate bracket sets and Buddhist statues housed inside. Over the past decades, experts from China’s State Administration of Cultural Heritage have repeatedly expressed concern about wind damage, seismic vulnerability and accumulated tilting, arguing that the centuries‑old timber system requires careful long‑term intervention to prevent irreversible loss.

Heritage conservation practice in China, as described by specialists working on sites like the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, places strong emphasis on pre‑dismantling documentation of each structural member. Accounts of preparatory work refer to close inspection of timber joints, digital tagging of individual beams and columns, and the creation of detailed three‑dimensional records before any major intervention proceeds. According to conservation professionals, such work is intended to record information about original carpentry techniques, deformation patterns and hidden decay, thereby guiding later reinforcement or reconstruction efforts while complying with national and UNESCO‑inspired principles on the treatment of ancient wooden architecture.

【网传“应县木塔即将全部拆卸落地大修”不实】近期，有自媒体发文称“应县木塔即将全部拆卸落地（架）大修”，引发网民议论。对此，中国互联网联合辟谣平台向有关主管部门核实，确认相关信息系谣言。 应县木塔始建于辽代，是我国现存最高、体量最大、年代最古老的木结构楼阁式塔，是全国重点文物保护单位。目前，有关方面正在开展木塔变形监测、全面测绘与信息模型搭建，推进局部加固工作。网络上出现“应县木塔即将全部拆卸落地大修”信息均为谣言。 中国互联网联合辟谣平台提醒广大网民，关于应县木塔的保护与修缮信息，请以国家文物局、山西省文物局及当地官方渠道发布的消息为准，切勿轻信和传播谣言。（来源：中国互联网联合辟谣平台）

The reported conservation plan for the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda is being linked by some experts to a series of recent surveys, digital mapping efforts and structural assessments, according to cultural heritage researchers. Over the past few years, specialised teams have conducted on-site measurements, three-dimensional scanning and material analysis aimed at building a detailed profile of the monument’s condition. In some accounts, these activities are presented as steps leading towards a possible dismantling project, although specialist circles note that such data are routinely gathered to inform a range of preservation options.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post presents the dismantling plan as an approved, active reality with specific operational details, whereas the evidence explicitly identifies this claim as a verified rumor and states that only local reinforcement and monitoring are currently underway.
text_analysis.t1_evidence: The post claims 'Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling' and describes active 'site preparations' and 'cranes and scaffold towers', but evidence id=factcheck_025 explicitly states '网络上出现“应县木塔即将全部拆卸落地大修”信息均为谣言' (Information about the imminent dismantling is a rumor) and that authorities are currently '推进局部加固工作' (advancing local reinforcement work), not dismantling.
t1_problematic_subclaims: Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul
site preparations, access controls and materials planning are understood to be under way
Cover image: A wide view of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda shows cranes and scaffold towers positioned around the upper tiers as preservation teams prepare for the staged dismantling
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post frames the event as an ongoing, approved project with active construction ('cranes and scaffold towers positioned... as preservation teams prepare'), while evidence id=news_025_7 clarifies the context is a debunked rumor and the actual current context is '推进局部加固工作' (advancing local reinforcement work) and monitoring, not dismantling.
t3_problematic_subclaims: The post presents the dismantling as an approved and active phase ('approved under the overhaul plan', 'start after final site mobilization'), whereas the evidence establishes the context as a false rumor circulating online while actual work is limited to local reinforcement.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is a high-confidence fabrication that treats a debunked rumor as established fact, inventing specific operational details and active construction scenes that contradict the ground truth of local reinforcement and official denials.

images.scene_description: A low-angle shot of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda surrounded by scaffolding and two cranes, with construction workers and stacked timber beams in the foreground.
images.explanation: A clean, authentic news photo depicting the site preparations described in the text.

images.scene_description: A view of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda with scaffolding on the sides, a crane arm visible on the right, and large, carved wooden beams resting on the ground in the foreground.
images.ocr_text: 文物保护 修缮工地 闲人免入
images.explanation: A genuine photo illustrating the ground-level restoration phase mentioned in the text.

images.scene_description: A high-angle, wide aerial view of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda complex, showing the tower, surrounding courtyards, trees, and distant landscape.
images.explanation: A real photo of the pagoda, but the caption falsely claims it shows cranes and scaffolds which are not present.

images.scene_description: Three conservation workers in white hard hats and blue jackets are inspecting the wooden structure of the pagoda, using a laser scanner and clipboards.
images.explanation: A clear, authentic photo showing the conservation work described in the text.

image_analysis.global_notes: Image 3 is the only problematic image; it is a real photo but the caption makes a false claim about the presence of cranes and scaffolding, which are not visible in the scene.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子的核心主张——应县木塔即将全部拆卸落地大修——已被官方辟谣平台确认为谣言，而配图虽然展示了真实的修缮现场（如脚手架、工人），却被错误地用于支持这一虚假的‘全面拆卸’叙事，导致图文语境严重不符。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post claims a full dismantling is approved, but evidence [11] explicitly calls this a rumor. The images show real maintenance work, but the text frames it as a specific dismantling project, creating a mismatch.
judge.core_claim: Chinese authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda, with active site preparations underway.
key_subclaims: Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda.
The project involves active site preparations, including cranes, scaffolding, and stacked timber sections.
The dismantling phase is expected to start after final site mobilization and protective works are finished.
The project is likely to last several years and will involve temporary closure or sharply reduced access.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: Chinese cultural heritage authorities have approved a full dismantling and ground-level overhaul of the Yingxian Wooden Pagoda.
subclaim_findings.note: The official fact check explicitly states that the claim of 'full dismantling' is a rumor and that the actual work is limited to local reinforcement and monitoring.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The project involves active site preparations, including cranes, scaffolding, and stacked timber sections.
subclaim_findings.note: While the images show these elements, the fact check clarifies that the context of 'full dismantling' is false; the images likely depict general maintenance or reinforcement work, not the specific dismantling project described.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The dismantling phase is expected to start after final site mobilization and protective works are finished.
subclaim_findings.note: Since the dismantling project itself is a rumor, the timeline for its start is also fabricated.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The project is likely to last several years and will involve temporary closure or sharply reduced access.
subclaim_findings.note: The multi-year timeline and closure are consequences of the non-existent full dismantling plan.

verdict_reasoning: The post's core claim that authorities have 'approved a full dismantling' is directly contradicted by official fact-check evidence (factcheck_025) which labels it a rumor.

The images show real construction/monitoring activities consistent with evidence descriptions (scaffolding, workers, timber), but they are misused to support the false narrative of a full dismantling project.
This creates a T1 Fabrication label for the text and a C2 Contextual Inconsistency label for the image-text binding.
Since the core claim is a verified rumor and the images are used to mislead about the nature of the work, the post is False.
Anti-bias rule applied: T1 Fabrication with C2 Contextual Inconsistency leads to False verdict.

image_findings.explanation: The image shows construction activity (cranes, scaffolding) which aligns with the visual description of 'protective scaffolding' in evidence, but the post frames this as 'full dismantling' which is a false claim (T1), creating a contextual mismatch (C2).

The image shows stacked timber and scaffolding, consistent with evidence descriptions of 'suspended timber segments' and 'temporary working platforms', but the post's narrative of 'full dismantling' is a fabrication (T1), leading to a contextual mismatch (C2).

The image is a general aerial view of the pagoda complex, which matches the evidence description of the site, but the post uses it to illustrate a specific 'dismantling' phase that is a rumor (T1), creating a contextual mismatch (C2).

The image shows workers inspecting the structure with tools, which aligns with evidence descriptions of 'monitoring' and 'digital tagging', but the post frames this as preparation for 'full dismantling' which is a false claim (T1), creating a contextual mismatch (C2).
